Title: N6-methyladenosine modifications stabilize phosphate starvation response–related mRNAs in plant adaptation to nutrient-deficient stress

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: m6A writer; METTL3 ortholog; S-adenosyl-L-methionine-dependent RNA methyltransferase
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential macronutrient for photosynthesis, nucleic acids, post-translational modification, and biosynthesis of essential metabolites
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MTA positively regulates Pi homeostasis and systemic phosphate starvation response signaling; MTA knockdown reduces global m6A, lowers shoot Pi concentration, and attenuates expression of PHR1 and downstream Pi starvation genes under Pi deficiency.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MTA is a core m6A writer required for normal global m6A abundance in Arabidopsis seedlings. 2. amiR-mta reduced MTA expression to ~25% of WT and lowered the m6A/A ratio to ~40% of WT under both +P and -P. 3. MTA knockdown decreased shoot Pi concentration and weakened systemic PSR signaling under Pi deficiency. 4. Reduced MTA shortened the half-lives of Pi starvation-induced m6A-hypermethylated PSD genes and of PHR1 mRNA.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: artificial microRNA knockdown; LC-MS/MS quantification of m6A; RNA-seq; transcriptome-wide RNA decay assay; RT-qPCR; physiological measurement of shoot Pi concentration and root growth; chemical inhibition with STM2457
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in seedlings; transcript level reduced in amiR-mta; no major Pi-starvation-induced change in WT reported
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pi deficiency (-P); normal Pi (+P); STM2457 treatment
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: MTA knockdown caused inhibited growth, reduced biomass, shorter primary roots, and aggravated growth inhibition under Pi deficiency
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to enhance phosphate-use adaptation by preserving m6A-dependent PSR signaling, but strong reduction causes growth penalties so partial/tissue-specific tuning would be required
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: m6A-seq; RNA-seq; transcriptome-wide RNA decay RNA-seq; PAS-seq2; GSA: CRA016391
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as Common Gene because its primary role in this paper is RNA methylation machinery rather than classical metabolic enzyme or TF; MTA abundance itself was not strongly altered by Pi status or phr1 phl1 mutation.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: GARP family TF with MYB-like DNA-binding domain and coiled-coil domain
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential macronutrient for photosynthesis, nucleic acids, post-translational modification, and biosynthesis of essential metabolites
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Systemic phosphate starvation response signaling and Pi homeostasis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PHR1 promotes Pi starvation-induced m6A deposition and systemic PSR signaling; m6A modification stabilizes PHR1 mRNA in a positive feedback loop, enabling stronger expression of PSR genes and improved Pi homeostasis under Pi deficiency.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: transcriptional activation of PSR genes; positive feedback via interaction with m6A writers leading to m6A-dependent stabilization of its own mRNA
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates downstream PSR genes; promotes expression of PHF1, PHO1, PHT1;1/PT1, ACP5 and other PSI genes under Pi deficiency
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Forms homodimers or heterodimers with PHL1; interacts with MTA and MTB in nuclei under -P
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Positive feedback loop: Pi starvation induces PHR-mediated m6A on PHR1 transcript, which stabilizes PHR1 mRNA and facilitates PSI signaling
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: phr1 phl1 mutant m6A-seq; RNA-seq; Y2H; BiFC; nuclear Co-IP; transgenic complementation in phr1 with WT vs m6A-loci-mutated PHR1 3'UTR; target-specific RNA decay assay; RT-qPCR; anthocyanin and Pi-response phenotyping
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in seedlings; transcript steady-state level reduced in amiR-mta and STM2457-treated plants under -P
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native PHR1 promoter in complementation constructs
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pi deficiency (-P); normal Pi (+P); cordycepin in RNA decay assay
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: phr1 or m6A-site-mutated PHR1 showed reduced anthocyanin accumulation and weaker PSI gene induction under Pi deficiency, implying compromised adaptation to low Pi
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A high-value regulatory target for improving phosphate starvation adaptation; preserving or enhancing m6A-stabilized PHR1 transcripts may strengthen PSR signaling, but excessive pathway activation may need balancing with growth
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: m6A RNA modification in the 3'UTR stabilizes PHR1 mRNA; six of seven putative m6A loci were experimentally validated by SELECT and abolished by A-to-U mutagenesis
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: m6A-seq; RNA-seq; transcriptome-wide RNA decay RNA-seq; PAS-seq2; GSA: CRA016391
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Master regulator of systemic PSR and a major controller of Pi-starvation-induced m6A remodeling; its own mRNA stabilization is decisive for full PSI signaling output
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PHR1 is a central PSR regulator whose transcript acquires Pi-starvation-induced m6A in the 3'UTR. 2. Loss of PHR1/PHL1 largely abolishes global PSI-m6A induction under -P. 3. PHR1 physically interacts with m6A writers MTA and MTB in nuclei under -P. 4. Mutation of PHR1 3'UTR m6A sites reduces PHR1 transcript stability, weakens complementation of phr1, lowers anthocyanin accumulation, and decreases downstream PSI gene expression.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PHRs did not mediate PSI-m6A primarily through direct transcriptional regulation of m6A-marked genes or writer abundance changes; APA shifts did not explain PHR1 stabilization.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: GARP family TF with MYB-like DNA-binding domain and coiled-coil domain
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential macronutrient for photosynthesis, nucleic acids, post-translational modification, and biosynthesis of essential metabolites
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Systemic phosphate starvation response signaling and Pi starvation-induced m6A remodeling
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PHL1 acts with PHR1 to mediate much of the Pi starvation-induced increase in m6A, thereby supporting PSR signaling under Pi deficiency.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Co-regulatory transcription factor function with PHR1; contributes to PHR-mediated induction of PSI-m6A
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Supports induction of PSI gene expression and PHR-mediated PSI-m6A dynamics
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: phr1 phl1 double-mutant m6A-seq and RNA-seq comparative analysis under +P and -P
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss together with PHR1 diminishes PSI-m6A induction and weakens PSR-associated transcript regulation
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential co-target with PHR1 for engineering improved low-phosphate adaptation, likely best considered in regulatory module tuning
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Indirect involvement in m6A-associated regulatory feedback through PHR module; no direct PHL1 m6A mechanistic validation reported
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important co-regulator with PHR1 for Pi-starvation-induced epitranscriptomic remodeling
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PHL1, together with PHR1, is required for most Pi-starvation-induced m6A increases. 2. The phr1 phl1 double mutant largely suppresses PSI-m6A peaks under -P. 3. This supports a central role of the PHR module in coordinating PSR transcriptional and epitranscriptomic responses.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core evidence is genetic through the phr1 phl1 double mutant; direct physical interaction with writers was shown for PHR1, not specifically for PHL1.
